title: Removable Storage — USB Mount Events on Engineering Workstations
exclusions: Authorised after-hours access per approved rosters; maintenance contractor scheduled access; emergency response events; seasonal pattern variations; high-throughput lobby areas by design.
evidence: Physical security monitoring report showing access events, anomaly investigations, camera system health, and incident correlation for security perimeter validation.

compliance.assurance_rationale: ISO/IEC 27001 A.7.10 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that ISO 27001 A.7.10 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.6.23: Removable Storage — USB Mount Events on Engineering Workstations.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.6.23' running on Sysmon EventCode=22, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Generate a test badge access event for a restricted area at an unauthorised time (3 AM on a non-scheduled day) for a user without after-hours authorisation; verify the physical security alert fires within 5 minutes.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Generate a test badge access event for a restricted area during authorised hours for a user with documented access approval; verify no physical security alert fires.

spl:
index=endpoint sourcetype=XmlWinEventLog:Microsoft-Windows-Sysmon/Operational EventCode=22 earliest=-30d
| lookup restricted_subnets.csv src_ip OUTPUT zone
| where zone="engineering_secure"
| stats count by ComputerName, Image
description: Uses endpoint logs for removable media mounts in restricted VLANs to enforce media handling procedures.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for ISO 27001 compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes endpoint and sourcetypes XmlWinEventLog:Microsoft-Windows-Sysmon/Operational. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) USB policy allowlist; (2) DLP correlation optional; (3) quarterly sample for auditors. Indexes required: `index=endpoint`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=XmlWinEventLog:Microsoft-Windows-Sysmon/Operational`; Lookups: `restricted_subnets`, `restricted_subnets.csv`.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorised after-hours access** — Staff with documented after-hours authorisation (on-call, operations) generating access events outside business hours per approved rosters.

2. **Maintenance contractor access** — Facilities maintenance personnel with scheduled access during documented maintenance windows and visitor management records.

3. **Emergency response activities** — Access events during fire drills, medical emergencies, or facilities emergencies documented in the incident log.

4. **Seasonal patterns** — Access pattern changes during office events, holiday periods, or building configuration changes documented in facilities management.

5. **Tailgating vs. multiple-person entry** — Access control systems detecting multiple persons per badge swipe in lobby areas designed for high throughput.

**Operational suppression:** Maintain a lookup table (`iso27001_approved_exceptions.csv`) mapping known legitimate activities by control owner, risk acceptance reference, and expiry date. Filter the SPL results against this lookup to suppress documented exceptions and reduce alert fatigue. Review and rotate entries at each ISMS management review cycle.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `endpoint` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Lookups**: `restricted_subnets.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('restricted_subnets.csv')`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `A.7.10`. The control objective is: "Evidence that ISO 27001 A.7.10 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.6.23: Removable Storage — USB Mount Events on Engineering Workstations." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `endpoint` under `XmlWinEventLog:Microsoft-Windows-Sysmon`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


Three operational considerations, authored alongside this UC, apply:


• **Hint 1:** USB policy allowlist. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** DLP correlation optional. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** quarterly sample for auditors. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "Removable Storage — USB Mount Events on Engineering Workstations," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the ISO 27001 control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time.

This search enriches raw events with context from the lookup `restricted_subnets.csv` — the enrichment table that adds compliance-relevant business context to raw events. The raw event stream alone cannot answer the compliance question because it does not carry the authoritative business attributes (`zone`) — these live in a register maintained outside the monitored system. The `| lookup` joins at search time on `src_ip`, making the search results only as accurate as the lookup's freshness.

Lookup ownership and freshness: This is the single most common failure mode for lookup-based compliance searches. The `restricted_subnets.csv` table must be owned by a named team with a documented refresh cadence and staleness alerting. A minimum operational SLA is: (1) lookup refreshed at a cadence matching the risk domain (daily for access control, weekly for inventory, quarterly for vendor registers), (2) automated alert when the lookup has not been rewritten in 2x the refresh interval, (3) a row-count sanity check (alert if row count changes by more than 20% between refreshes — catches truncation bugs in upstream exports).

Case sensitivity: Splunk's lookup matching is case-sensitive by default. If the raw event field and the lookup key differ in case (e.g. `Account_Name=JSMITH` vs lookup `samaccountname=jsmith`), the join silently returns null. Normalize both sides with `| eval src_ip = lower(src_ip)` before the lookup, or set `case_sensitive_match = false` in the lookup's `transforms.conf` stanza. Search across the last 30 days.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.6.23: Removable Storage — USB Mount Events on Engineering Workstat]
cron_schedule = 0 3 1 * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -30d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.6.23,reg=ISO_27001
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=endpoint sourcetype=XmlWinEventLog:Microsoft-Windows-Sysmon earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `ComputerName`, `Image`. Run:

```spl
index=endpoint sourcetype=XmlWinEventLog:Microsoft-Windows-Sysmon earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table ComputerName, Image
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `restricted_subnets.csv`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup restricted_subnets.csv
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.6.23: ISO 27001 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Drill-through table (the key fields):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 2 — Distribution (the key fields):** Shows the volumetric distribution across the primary dimension. Use `sort` so the worst offender is first — auditors focus on the tallest bar.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** Check the lookup's case sensitivity (Splunk lookups are case-sensitive by default) and null handling — a null on the input side produces a silent miss, not an error.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**ISO 27001-specific failure modes:**

• Statement of Applicability (SoA) drift: ISO 27001 requires a SoA listing which Annex A controls are applicable and which are excluded with justification. A detection for a control excluded in the SoA adds noise to the audit evidence pile; reconcile UC-to-SoA mapping annually. Maintain `iso27001_soa.csv` with current applicability.

• Context of the organisation (Cl. 4): controls calibrated for a 50-person company's threat model do not suit a 5000-person multinational's. Detection thresholds, log retention, and escalation paths must align with the organisation's ISMS scope — a single 'enterprise default' for ISO 27001 is a tell-tale sign of a cookie-cutter implementation.

• Internal audit programme (Cl. 9.2) evidence: detections that support internal audits must produce records that can be attached to audit evidence packs. Tag the alert payload with audit_cycle=YYYY-HN so evidence bundling is trivial.

• Management review inputs (Cl. 9.3): control effectiveness metrics must be reviewable at management review. A detection that only raises alerts (no trend metric) cannot be brought to the management meeting; schedule a companion report that produces trend data per control.
grandmaExplanation: We keep evidence for Removable Storage — USB Mount Events on Engineering Workstations. ISO 27001 tells us what good security management looks like, and we document that we follow it.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Endpoint.Processes by Processes.process_name, Processes.user, Processes.dest | sort - count
